message.publisher: American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS)
message.abstract:
<jats:title>How cow genomes have moo-ved</jats:title>
          <jats:p>
            Cattle were domesticated ∼10,000 years ago, but analysis of modern breeds has not elucidated their origins. Verdugo
            <jats:italic>et al.</jats:italic>
            performed genome-wide analysis of 67 ancient Near Eastern
            <jats:italic>Bos taurus</jats:italic>
            DNA samples. Several populations of ancient aurochs were progenitors of domestic cows. These genetic lineages mixed ∼4000 years ago in a region around the Indus Valley. Interestingly, mitochondrial analysis indicated that genetic material likely derived from arid-adapted
            <jats:italic>Bos indicus</jats:italic>
            (zebu) bulls was introduced by introgression.
          </jats:p>
          <jats:p>
            <jats:italic>Science</jats:italic>
            , this issue p.
            <jats:related-article xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="doi" issue="6449" page="173" related-article-type="in-this-issue" vol="365" xlink:href="10.1126/science.aav1002">173</jats:related-article>
          </jats:p>
title: Ancient cattle genomics, origins, and rapid turnover in the Fertile Crescent
